data.title: Tesla faces an uphill climb to match last year's Q2 deliveries tally
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) delivered 443,956 vehicles in Q2 of 2024, which is a mark that the company may struggle to match in Q2 of this year. Following the company's weak Q1 deliveries report, analysts and prediction markets now expect deliveries to range between 350,000 and 375,000 vehicles, reflecting a potential year-over-year decline once again.

The drop is attributed partly to the Model Y's production changeover, but other issues persist. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) ended the quarter with record high inventory levels and no backlog for the new Model Y, which is readily available in many U.S. regions. Of course, that scenario contrasts with prior years, when Tesla's (TSLA) demand often exceeded supply.

While Tesla (TSLA) remains the U.S. electric vehicle leader, the company's dominant market share continues to slip amid competition from brands such as Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Lucid Group (LCID), Ford Motor (F), General Motors (GM), Honda (HMC), Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF) and Ford Motor (F). Analysts think the decline reflects broader challenges for Tesla (TSLA), including public backlash against CEO Elon Musk and the intensifying competition in the EV market. The upshot is that Tesla (TSLA) could struggle to match last year's deliveries tally unless there is a strong response to new model introductions (Model 2 or Model Q) and the refreshed Model Y.

data.title: European, US chip stocks gain amid temporary reprieve from Trump's tariffs
data.description:
Several European and U.S. chip stocks were in the green premarket on Monday after the Trump administration temporarily exempted electronics such as smartphones and computers, among other items, mostly imported from China, from reciprocal tariffs.

AI chipmakers Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) both jumped around 2%, each. In addition, Broadcom (AVGO) and Qualcomm (QCOM) climbed about 1%, each.

The exclusions were published on Friday by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency and provided a big relief to American tech giants such as Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA) and Dell Technologies  (DELL). However, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said that it was a temporary reprieve and added that, "all those products are going to come under semiconductors, and they're going to have a special focus type of tariff to make sure that those products get reshored."

European chip stocks such as Dutch semiconductor equipment maker ASML (ASML) was up about 1% while French-Italian STMicroelectronics (STM) was largely flat, but in the red, premarket on Monday. Shares of Netherlands-based NXP Semiconductors' (NXPI) rose about 2%, while Neubiberg-based Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY) (OTCQX:IFNNF) jumped about 3% in Germany.

On Friday, it was reported that China's import tariffs on U.S. goods provide an exemption for U.S. semiconductor companies that outsource their manufacturing. Reportedly, U.S. companies such as Qualcomm (QCOM) and AMD, which outsource manufacturing to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), are exempted as China's regulators classify these chips as originating from Taiwan.

Intel (INTC) and Micron (MU) surged around 4%, each premarket on Monday. GlobalFoundries (GFS) and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) and Marvell Technology (MRVL) each grew nearly 2%. Texas Instruments (TXN) rose about 1%, while Analog Devices (ADI) was largely flat, but in the green. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) was largely flat but in the red.

Meanwhile, chip equipment makers: Applied Materials (AMAT) and KLA (KLAC) each rose nearly 1%. Lam Research (LRCX) was up roughly 0.5%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Enghouse Systems acquires Trafi
data.description:
• Enghouse Systems (TSX:ENGH:CA) said it has acquired a provider of mobility-as-a-service solutions based in Lithuania, Trafi. 
  • "Trafi enhances our transportation mobility solutions portfolio by adding a robust MaaS platform that integrates multiple transport modes into a seamless user experience," said CEO, Steve Sadler.

data.title: XXIX Metal names Guy Le Bel as CEO
data.description:
• XXIX Metal (OTCQB:QCCUF) said it has named Guy Le Bel as its chief executive officer.
  • Bel, who has over three decades of experience, will succeed Stephen Stewart in the role.
  • Stewart will remain actively involved with the firm as chair and strategic advisor.
  • The firm also appointed Denis McNichols to vice president, exploration, succeeding Charles Beaudry.

data.title: M&T Bank Non-GAAP EPS of $3.38 misses by $0.02, revenue of $2.32B misses by $30M
data.description:
• M&T Bank press release (NYSE:MTB): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $3.38 misses by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $2.32B (+0.9% Y/Y) misses by $30M.
  • Net interest margin widened to 3.66% in the recent quarter as compared with 3.58% in the fourth quarter of 2024 reflecting lower levels of average earning assets.
  • The provision for credit losses was $130 million in the first quarter of 2025 as compared with $140 million in the immediately preceding quarter and $200 million in the first quarter of 2024.

data.title: Accenture invests in GoUpscale
data.description:
• Accenture (NYSE:ACN) has made an investment in content engagement technology company, GoUpscale.
  • Terms of the investment were not disclosed.
  • GoUpscale will join Accenture Ventures’ Project Spotlight accelerator for data and AI companies. The two companies will help wealth management firms create personalized communications and meaningful client experiences.

data.title: Stock index futures gain after Trump's exempts tech from reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
Stock index futures rode high on Monday, as President Donald Trump announced temporary tariff exemptions on smartphones, computers, and other electronics primarily imported from China.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +1%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +1.2%, and Dow futures (INDU) +0.9%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 2 basis points to 4.47%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 1 basis point to 3.94%.

Trump's administration on Friday exempted smartphones, computers, and other electronics mostly imported from China from reciprocal tariffs, providing major relief for American tech players.

Wall Street on Friday overcame a volatile morning trading session to push higher, ending a wild week on a positive note. Tariffs dominated headlines, with the latest being that the Trump administration had made "very good progress" on trade negotiations.

"Tariffs continued to dominate headlines over the weekend, starting with news late on Friday that smartphones, computers, and some other electronics would be exempt from U.S. reciprocal tariffs," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said.

Turning to the week ahead, while tariffs will remain the center of attention, investors will also be keeping an eye on incoming U.S. data with the March retail sales and industrial production prints on Wednesday, Sidorov added.

"Over the weekend, Trump partially retreated from the retreat, emphasizing that a 20% tax would apply and additional unspecified taxes on technology are coming. This erratic policy making leads to investors questioning the existence of a competent plan," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The week ahead will also see a slew of big companies reporting their earnings, including Bank of America (BAC), Netflix (NFLX), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and American Express (AXP).

The economic calendar is on the lighter side today, with mainly the Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic and Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin's speeches on tap.

data.title: Fortuna announces sale of non-core San Jose Mine, Mexico
Commodity Roundup: UBS slashes Brent forecast by $12/bbl as trade war weighs on oil prices
data.description:
U.S. tariffs and the trade war between the United States and China will likely weigh on economic growth this year and are likely to result in oil demand growing at a slower pace, UBS analysts said on Monday.

As such, the bank reduced its Brent forecasts by $12/bbl to $68-bbl, and expects WTI to trade at $64/bbl.

"There’s a high positive correlation between U.S. equities and crude oil prices at present, which usually indicates that demand factors are driving prices," Giovanni Staunovo, Strategist, UBS Switzerland AG said in a note.

Such a high correlation was seen during the global financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic.

"Some indicators, such as the futures curve being in backwardation (i.e., downward sloped), suggest that the oil market remains tight, but tariffs together with the ongoing uncertainty over theUS administration's next steps—that is, which tariffs are added next—are likely to weigh on economic growth."

Hence, UBS reduces it global oil demand growth forecast by 0.4mbpd to 0.8mbpd for 2025, as lower oil prices are also likely to reduce U.S. oil supply growth slightly this year.

Goldman Sachs meanwhile, expects oil prices to decline through the end of 2025 and into 2026, with Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI (CL1:COM) averaging $63 and $59 per barrel, respectively, this year and falling to $58 and $55 next year, due to rising recession risks and increased OPEC+ supply.

West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) crude futures were up +0.83% to $62.03 at the time of writing, while Brent crude (CO1:COM), the international benchmark, was +0.78% at $65.26 a barrel and has declined over 13% YTD.

Elsewhere, China’s commodity imports mostly fell year-on-year in March, with declines in coal (XAL1:COM), natural gas (NG1:COM), iron ore (SCO:COM), and soybean (S_1:COM).

Crude oil imports surged 20% to a 19-month-high, driven by record Iranian flows and stockpiling by private refiners due to concerns over US sanctions.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (LE)

China ETFs: (KWEB), (PGJ), (FXI), (ASHR), (YIN), (YANG), (GXC), (FXP), (KBA), (CXSE), (CNYA), (CHAU), (FLCH), (YXI), (FCA), (KALL), (JCHI)

data.title: Biggest stock movers Monday: Tech stocks, and more
data.description:
Stock futures rose Monday morning after Trump’s new “reciprocal” tariffs exempted tech products like smartphones and chips, though officials warned the exemptions may not last.

Here are some of Monday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) +5.5% – Tech stocks saw a surge after the Trump administration temporarily excluded smartphones, laptops, and key electronics components from its new 145% reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods. The U.S. Customs and Border Protection issued the exemptions late Friday, providing relief to tech stocks, though both President Trump and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned that these exemptions may not be permanent. Other notable gainers included Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) +3%, Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) +7%, Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) +5.7%, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) +5%, and HP (NYSE:HPQ) +6%.
  • BP (NYSE:BP) +4% - Shares rose after the company announced the discovery of oil at the U.S. Far South prospect in the Gulf of Mexico. The discovery, which was made approximately 120 miles off the Louisiana coast at a depth of 23,830 feet, is part of BP’s strategy to increase its upstream production. Initial data suggests the presence of high-quality reservoirs and potentially commercial volumes of hydrocarbons. BP's revised strategy aims to increase its global upstream production to between 2.3M and 2.5M barrels of oil equivalent per day by 2030, with plans for further growth through 2035. The company also intends to drill around 40 wells over the next three years, with 10 to 15 wells expected to be drilled in 2025, underscoring its renewed focus on exploration.
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +2.5% – Shares rose after the company announced it would not sell Polish broadcaster TVN, putting an end to speculation surrounding the bidding process. In a statement on Monday, WBD explained that, following a strategic review of options for TVN, it had concluded that retaining ownership was the best path forward. The company emphasized its commitment to supporting its business and strategy, along with the journalistic efforts of its team. The decision comes amid financial market volatility triggered by U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff policies, which have caused many investors to pause deal-making activities.
  • DuPont de Nemours (DD) +3% – Shares climbed after KeyBanc upgraded the stock to Overweight from Sector Weight, citing the company’s strong balance sheet as a key factor in its ability to navigate a potential economic downturn. Analyst Aleksey Yefremov highlighted DuPont’s electronics and water segments as "world-class secular growth franchises" that are currently trading at unusually discounted valuations.
